**[17:21](https://youtu.be/gQTsGo8c0WQ?t=1041)** — - Good evening. My name is Angie Rogers and I live in Coralville. I'm here tonight as a community stakeholder regarding the financial crisis facing our district. I wanna remind the board of something fundamental. You are not a rubber stamp for district administration. You are an elected governing body and your job is oversight. By law and public trust you are responsible for overseeing the superintendent and safeguarding district finances on behalf of students, staff, taxpayers, and community. Director Williams and Malone were recently quoted in the "Gazette" saying, quote, "The board bears this responsibility. We are fiduciaries of the district and we have to do a better job." End of quote. And that quote, in hindsight, end of quote, the board could have asked more questions.

**[18:06](https://youtu.be/gQTsGo8c0WQ?t=1086)** — Acknowledging mistakes after the fact is not sufficient. This board must exercise rigorous oversight in real time. The core fiduciary duties of a board member are: duty of care, meaning be diligent and informed; duty of loyalty, put the community and district as a whole first; and the duty to follow law and district policy. When the retroactive approval of the $10 million interfund transfer was discussed and voted on, every board member had the duty to probe administration's request. Yet the public watched as only two board members asked meaningful questions about how a transfer that size happened without board approval. This is unacceptable governance and oversight. I also want to address accountability and fairness. Board members are elected to represent the community

**[18:53](https://youtu.be/gQTsGo8c0WQ?t=1133)** — and make hard decisions even when those decisions are uncomfortable. When there are close relationships to the district, including spouses employed by the district or close ties to senior leadership, the board must take extra care to avoid even the appearance as conflicted oversight. Please ask the basic governance questions family, staff, and taxpayers expect regarding this former CFO and the executive cabinet compensation decisions. How many candidates applied for the open CFO position? If there was more than one, what were their qualifications? Who did the CFO report to? What oversight of this position occurred over the last three years? Were there any annual reviews given? That chain of accountability must be examined openly and thoroughly. If you don't know where to start to look,

**[19:39](https://youtu.be/gQTsGo8c0WQ?t=1179)** — look at the organizational chart provided by district leadership, which can be found in the March 3rd, 2026 slide deck. In addition, how are compensation decisions made at the executive cabinet level specifically. The public deserves clarity on how executive cabinet raises are determined, who approves them, and what controls or oversight exist to ensure those decisions are fair, consistent, and aligned with budget realities. Given that the superintendent is the only employee that reports to the board, I believe that the public deserves transparency on these matters. Finally, I urge you to vote no tonight on reducing classroom staffing. The proposed cuts total $2.2 million. It is unacceptable for district leadership to remain largely insulated from these cuts

**[20:24](https://youtu.be/gQTsGo8c0WQ?t=1224)** — while also proposing classroom staff reductions. In addition, Central Administration seems to continue to add to its headcount during this crisis. The board documents for tonight include a recommendation for a salary adjustment for a February 2026 hire of an enrollment and marketing specialist with an annual salary over $53,000 plus benefits. We are in a financial crisis. Why is there not a hiring freeze at Central Administration? You are elected to lead, not defer. Our community expects diligence, independence, and accountability. Thank you. - Thank you. (attendees clap) Next up, we will have Elisa Meeks.

**[21:15](https://youtu.be/gQTsGo8c0WQ?t=1275)** — - Hello, my name is Elisa Meeks and I'm here to speak to the board tonight about the budget and class sizes. I'm a proud West High graduate who has three children who attend Iowa City schools. My own children attended Kirkwood Elementary. This is also my 22nd year as an elementary teacher in the Iowa City Community School District. I love teaching in Iowa City and have had the privilege to teach at Horn, Wickham, Lemme, Online Program, and now Twain. I was a district mentor during the 2018-2019 school year and traveled to Grant Wood, Hills, Twain, Hoover, and Lucas. As you can see, I have been in many places, seen a lot of classes, and taught everything from kindergarten to fourth grade. Anyone who tells you class size does not matter

**[22:00](https://youtu.be/gQTsGo8c0WQ?t=1320)** — or have serious impacts on learning has not been in a classroom. It is one of the highest contributing factors to how a student's school year is going to go. A teaching colleague of mine who has 33 students this year sums up these challenges perfectly. Less individualized instruction. It's more difficult to narrow down skill gaps. Struggling students get less intervention or intense intervention due to large groups. Advanced students get lost in the mix and small reading and math groups are shorted. The logistical challenges of trying to meet with 30+ readers in small groups daily is nearly impossible to tackle. Reduced quality and speed of feedback. Quality feedback is one of the most research supported teaching moves,

**[22:46](https://youtu.be/gQTsGo8c0WQ?t=1366)** — yet it suffers greatly with large class sizes. It takes longer to give written feedback to each child and to grade assignments, lengthening the window between what they hear and when they did their work. This makes the feedback less effective. Teacher workload. Large classes drastically increase teacher workload. For example, it took hours outside of contract time to fully prepare for parent-teacher conferences. We don't have enough prep time to do the work the job requires us to do. Oftentimes there are not even 30+ conference slots available. Additionally, behind-the-scenes paperwork, grading and responding to emails increases with more students. Last year I was a single section of fourth grade at Twain.

**[23:32](https://youtu.be/gQTsGo8c0WQ?t=1412)** — I started the year with 29 students and quickly went to 32. I even had 33 students in my room at one point. My classroom is not big enough to hold 33 students comfortably. There is no carpet space and barely enough room for 33 desks. The environment was frequently overstimulating to both students and adults because of the sheer number of bodies in such a small space. This year I have 28 and even that is still a lot of students for the size of the room. We all hate the thought of students sitting in their desks all day. Yet, we have no space to move around for brain breaks or transition from a circle or carpet area to desks for movement transitions. Now you are probably thinking,

**[24:18](https://youtu.be/gQTsGo8c0WQ?t=1458)** — "Elisa, we already support keeping class size low. That's why we have RAM allocations." Yes, and yet 32 students in a classroom is still too high. The elementary teaching load demand has never been higher. Still, this school year, a fourth grade classroom has 33 students from day one and never got a second section. This is a problem. I would argue that RAM-3 should not exist. It doesn't matter what building you teach in, 30 students, or to be clear, way less in a classroom should be the aspiration. Please know that elementary teachers in Iowa City have been asked to do more and more each and every year. We cover for each other. We miss our lunches, often have no one in the building for behavior support,

**[25:04](https://youtu.be/gQTsGo8c0WQ?t=1504)** — missed preps for meetings every single week. And still we show up with so much heart to try to make things awesome for our kids. Budget cuts clearly have to happen. However, I strongly encourage you to keep the money to do so closest to the kids. - Thank you. (attendees clap) Next up, we will have Maka Pilcher-Hayek.

**[1:09:37](https://youtu.be/gQTsGo8c0WQ?t=4177)** — Those are just a couple examples for you if you have any grants. **[1:36:37](https://youtu.be/gQTsGo8c0WQ?t=5797)** — and save 14 FTE. That's without closing a single school. **[2:08:57](https://youtu.be/gQTsGo8c0WQ?t=7737)** — Every voice matters and is respected and we are certainly trying to live that. Healing is part of restoring connection.
